title: God's Nature in Islam and Christianity
subtitle: Divine Attributes, Names, and the Nature of God in Islamic Tawhid and Christian Theology
summary: Islam and Christianity affirm God's fundamental attributes—omniscience, omnipotence, mercy, and justice—but differ in metaphysical frameworks, particularly regarding God's relationship to creation and the nature of divine unity. This article examines the 99 Names of Allah in Islamic theology and Christian conceptions of divine perfection, exploring philosophical approaches to understanding God's nature within each tradition.

seo.metaTitle: God's Nature in Islam and Christianity: Divine Attributes and the Trinity
seo.metaDescription: Comparative study of Islamic Tawhid and Christian theology: divine attributes, the 99 Names of Allah, the Trinity, and God's perfection in both traditions.

sections.title: Introduction: The Question of God's Nature
sections.body: Both Islam and Christianity begin with affirmation of monotheism: the belief in one God who is eternal, all-knowing, all-powerful, and uniquely worthy of worship. Yet they articulate God's nature through different philosophical and theological frameworks. Islam emphasizes Tawhid—the uncompromising oneness of Allah—expressed through His 99 revealed Names and Attributes. Christianity speaks of God the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit while maintaining monotheistic faith. These different formulations arise not from disagreement about God's fundamental perfections, but from distinct metaphysical systems and scriptural interpretations about how those attributes relate to creation, revelation, and divine self-manifestation. Understanding each tradition's internal logic reveals both profound areas of agreement and genuine theological differences that merit careful analysis.

sections.title: Islamic Understanding: Tawhid and the Divine Names
sections.body:
The Islamic concept of Tawhid—often translated as "the oneness of God"—stands as the central theological principle of Islam. Tawhid encompasses not only the belief that there is only one God, but also that this God is absolutely unique, without peers, partners, or intermediaries. God is neither created nor dependent on anything, while all creation is utterly dependent on Him.

Central to Islamic theology is the doctrine of Allah's Attributes, particularly the 99 Names (Al-Asma al-Husna—the Most Beautiful Names). These are not mere labels but represent distinct dimensions of God's nature and His relationship with creation. They include names describing His actions (the Creator, the Sustainer, the Judge), His attributes (the All-Knowing, the All-Powerful, the Merciful), and His transcendence (the Most High, the Most Great).

The Qur'an itself presents these divine names throughout its text, and Islamic theology emphasizes that all names derive from a single, unified divine nature. Al-Ghazali, the 11th-century theologian, explained that these names are not separate properties but different aspects through which humans can know and relate to the one God. For instance, Allah is simultaneously Al-Adl (The Just) and Ar-Rahman (The Merciful). These are not contradictory but complementary expressions of divine perfection. The tension between justice and mercy in human understanding is resolved in Islamic theology by affirming that both originate from a higher divine wisdom that humans cannot fully comprehend.

The Islamic approach emphasizes divine transcendence (tanzih). God is utterly unlike creation; He is not limited by time, space, or causality as humans are. This is why the Qur'an repeatedly states that "nothing is like unto Him" and warns against anthropomorphizing God. At the same time, the Qur'an speaks of God's intimacy with creation and those who seek Him. This balance between transcendence and immanence is fundamental to Islamic understanding of God's nature.

sections.title: Christian Understanding: Divine Perfection and the Trinity
sections.body:
Christian theology also affirms God's fundamental attributes—omniscience, omnipotence, omnibenevolence, and eternity—yet articulates these through the doctrine of the Trinity. Christianity maintains strict monotheism (the belief in one God) while holding that the one God exists eternally in three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. This is not, Christians argue, a contradiction to monotheism but a revelation of God's own internal reality.

Christian theology emphasizes that God is love (1 John 4:8). This is not one attribute among others but represents the very essence of God's being and action. The Christian understanding of God's nature is inseparable from the narrative of God's self-giving love expressed through creation, incarnation, redemption, and sanctification. God is described as omnibenevolent—not merely capable of love but constitutively loving. This divine love is understood as active, sacrificial, and relational.

The doctrine of divine omniscience in Christian theology means God possesses complete knowledge of all things—past, present, and future—simultaneously. God's omnipotence means God's power is unlimited and absolute, though Christian theology has grappled with questions about how this relates to human free will and the existence of evil. God's eternity means God exists outside time, transcending temporal limitations.

The Trinity articulates how God is one yet three. The Father is recognized as the source and sustainer of creation. The Son (Jesus Christ) is understood as the eternal Word of God made flesh, through whom creation exists and who mediates God's redemptive work. The Holy Spirit is understood as God's active presence in the world and in the hearts of believers. Christian theology emphasizes that these are not three gods but one God whose nature is fundamentally relational and self-giving.

Christian theology also speaks of God's perfection (as in Matthew 5:48, where Jesus teaches disciples to "be perfect, just as your Father in heaven is perfect"). This perfection encompasses all virtues—justice, mercy, holiness, wisdom, and love—unified in a single, perfect divine nature.

sections.title: Scripture and Divine Names
sections.body:
The Qur'an explicitly presents God's divine names and attributes in several key passages. Surah Al-Hashr (59:22-24) provides one of the most comprehensive presentations:

First, verse 59:22 establishes the foundational claim of Islamic monotheism: "He is Allah, other than whom there is no deity, Knower of the unseen and the witnessed. He is the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful." This verse emphasizes that God is unique, all-knowing, and characterized by mercy.

Verse 59:23 continues: "He is Allah, other than whom there is no deity, the Sovereign, the Pure, the Perfection, the Bestower of Faith, the Overseer, the Exalted in Might, the Compeller, the Superior. Exalted is Allah above whatever they associate with Him." This verse presents multiple divine names and attributes, affirming both God's absolute power and transcendence.

Verse 59:24 completes the passage: "He is Allah, the Creator, the Inventor, the Fashioner; to Him belong the best names. Whatever is in the heavens and earth is exalting Him. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise." Here the Qur'an emphasizes God's creative power, the perfection of His names, and universal acknowledgment of His greatness.

Another key Quranic passage is verse 20:8 from Surah Taha: "Allah—there is no deity except Him. To Him belong the best names." This verse succinctly affirms both God's absolute oneness and the perfection of His divine attributes.

The Throne Verse (Ayat al-Kursi) in verse 2:255 presents God's comprehensive knowledge and power: "Allah—there is no deity except Him, the Ever-Living, the Sustainer of [all] existence. Neither drowsiness overtakes Him nor sleep. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth." This verse emphasizes God's constant awareness and universal sovereignty.

In Christian scripture, the affirmation of divine perfection and nature appears in multiple forms. The passage 1 John 4:8 states: "He who doesn’t love doesn’t know God, for God is love." This verse presents love not merely as something God does but as constitutive of God's being.

1 Timothy 1:17 describes God in terms of transcendence and uniqueness: "Now to the King eternal, immortal, invisible, to God who alone is wise, be honor and glory forever and ever. Amen." This passage affirms God's eternal nature, incorporeality (invisibility), and unique wisdom.

The Johannine Prologue (John 1:1-3) presents divine nature through Logos theology: "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. The same was in the beginning with God. All things were made through him. Without him was not anything made that has been made." This passage articulates the Christian understanding that God's creative power and self-expression are eternally united in the Word.

Matthew 5:48 presents divine perfection as a model for human virtue: "Therefore you shall be perfect, just as your Father in heaven is perfect." This verse emphasizes that God's perfection encompasses all virtues to which humans are called to aspire.

sections.title: Philosophical Frameworks: Approaches to Understanding God
sections.body:
Both Islamic and Christian theology employ philosophical frameworks to articulate understanding of God, though with different emphases and starting points.

Islamic theology developed within a tradition that valued philosophical rigor, particularly through thinkers like Al-Kindi, Al-Farabi, Ibn Sina, and Al-Ghazali. These philosophers engaged with Greek metaphysics while grounding their thought in Quranic revelation. A central concern was maintaining the absolute transcendence and uniqueness of God (tawhid) while explaining how a transcendent God relates to creation.

The Islamic philosophical tradition emphasizes divine simplicity—the idea that God is completely unified without composition or parts. God's knowledge, power, justice, and mercy are not separate attributes added to God's essence; rather, they all constitute a single, unified divine reality. This stands in contrast to human understanding, where knowing something is different from willing it, which is different from being powerful. In God, Islamic philosophy argues, all these are one.

Islamic theology also emphasizes divine necessity. God exists necessarily—God must exist, and could not fail to exist. All creation, by contrast, is contingent—dependent on God for its existence. This metaphysical distinction grounds Islamic theology's insistence that only God is truly independent and eternal.

Christian theology, particularly in its Western medieval and scholastic forms, similarly grappled with philosophical questions about God's nature. Thinkers like Augustine, Thomas Aquinas, and Anselm employed Aristotelian and Neoplatonic philosophy to articulate Christian doctrine. However, Christian philosophy had to address the distinct challenge posed by the Incarnation and the Trinity.

Christian theology also affirms divine simplicity and necessity. God is pure being (ens per se), not a being among other beings. However, Christian theology uniquely had to explain how God's eternal nature relates to the temporal order while maintaining God's transcendence. The doctrine of divine omniscience addresses this: God knows all things in one eternal act of knowing, not sequentially as humans do. God's knowledge encompasses human free choices without causing them.

A key difference in philosophical approach involves how each tradition understands God's relationship to creation and time. Islamic theology, influenced by Neoplatonic philosophy, often emphasizes God's absolute transcendence and the radical dependence of creation. Some Islamic philosophers (particularly in the Ash'arite school) struggled with questions of human free will versus divine determinism, eventually emphasizing divine omnipotence and sovereignty to the point that human actions are seen as ultimately determined by God, though humans acquire responsibility for them.

Christian theology, particularly following Augustine and Aquinas, developed more nuanced positions on divine foreknowledge and human free will. Aquinas argued that God's knowledge is not temporal sequence but an eternal vision in which God sees all things as they truly are. This preserves both God's omniscience and genuine human agency. Later thinkers like Molina attempted to explain how God, through divine middle knowledge (scientia media), knows all possible human choices without determining them.

Both traditions also developed sophisticated theodicies to address the problem of evil. If God is omnipotent and good, why does evil exist? Islamic theology emphasizes divine wisdom beyond human comprehension and the role of free will. Christian theology similarly appeals to free will, the spiritual development that suffering can produce, and the eschatological resolution in God's final judgment and renewal of creation.

sections.title: Common Ground: Shared Divine Perfections
sections.body:
Despite their theological differences, Islam and Christianity affirm a substantial set of shared claims about God's nature:

1. Monotheism: Both traditions are strictly monotheistic, affirming belief in one and only one God. There is no god except Allah in Islamic faith; there is one God in Christianity, despite the Trinitarian understanding of God's nature.

2. Omniscience: Both traditions affirm that God possesses complete knowledge of all things. God is not ignorant, surprised, or limited in awareness. Islamic tradition emphasizes this through names like Al-Alim (The All-Knowing) and Al-Khaber (The All-Aware). Christian tradition equally affirms God's omniscience, including divine foreknowledge of all free choices.

3. Omnipotence: Both traditions hold that God's power is absolute and unlimited. Nothing can constrain or defeat God's will. Islamic theology emphasizes Allah's power through names like Al-Qawi (The Strong) and Al-Aziz (The Exalted in Might). Christian theology equally affirms God's almighty power.

4. Mercy and Compassion: Both traditions emphasize God's mercy and compassion toward creation. In Islam, Ar-Rahman and Ar-Rahim (The Merciful, The Compassionate) are so central that they open each surah of the Qur'an except one. In Christianity, divine mercy is central to the gospel narrative of redemption and forgiveness.

5. Justice: Both traditions affirm God's justice. God judges fairly and will hold creation accountable. Islamic tradition names this Al-Adl (The Just) and Al-Qasim (The Divider/Judge). Christian theology equally emphasizes God's just judgment.

6. Transcendence: Both traditions affirm that God is fundamentally different from and beyond creation. God is not material, not limited by time or space, and not subject to creation's laws. Islamic theology emphasizes this through the doctrine of tanzih (God's transcendence). Christian theology equally maintains God's transcendence, describing God as invisible, incorporeal, and eternal.

7. Eternity: Both traditions hold that God is eternal—not created, not subject to temporal change, and the source of all time and being. God was, is, and will be.

8. Uniqueness and Worthiness of Worship: Both traditions affirm that God alone is worthy of worship and obedience. All religious devotion rightly belongs to God, not to created beings or objects.

These shared affirmations represent substantial common ground between the two faiths and suggest that disagreements, while significant, concern how to understand and articulate these shared divine perfections rather than fundamental differences about what God is like.

sections.title: Theological Distinctions: Where Understanding Differs
sections.body:
While Islam and Christianity share affirmation of God's fundamental perfections, they differ significantly in how they understand and articulate God's nature and God's relationship to creation.

1. The Trinity and Christology: The most substantial theological difference concerns God's nature and self-revelation. Christianity holds that God exists eternally as three persons (Father, Son, Holy Spirit) in one divine nature. God's eternal Word became incarnate in Jesus Christ, fully human and fully divine. Islam explicitly rejects the idea that God is three in one or that God has a son. For Muslims, to say "God has a son" or that God is "three" violates the absolutely fundamental principle of tawhid. The idea that a human being (Jesus) could be divine or that God could have embodied himself in human form is seen in Islamic theology as incompatible with God's absolute transcendence and uniqueness.

From the Christian perspective, the Trinity is not an arbitrary addition to the concept of God but flows from the witness of scripture and the experience of God as Father, Son, and Spirit. The Incarnation is not a contradiction of God's transcendence but its supreme expression—God's radical self-giving love entering into human history and solidarity with creation.

2. Revelation and Prophethood: Islam holds that God reveals His will primarily through His word (the Qur'an), which came to Muhammad as God's final prophet. Christianity holds that God's ultimate revelation is personal—through Jesus Christ, the Word made flesh. Where Islam sees revelation as a message (the Qur'an), Christianity sees revelation as fundamentally a person (Christ). This difference affects how each tradition understands the status of their scriptures and the nature of God's communication with creation.

3. Divine Nature and Creation: Islamic theology, particularly in the Ash'arite school, tends to emphasize divine omnipotence and sovereignty to the point that God's will is absolutely determining. Creation has no independent agency or causal power; God creates each act. However, humans are held responsible for their actions—a paradox Islamic theology addresses through the concept of kasb (acquisition), where humans acquire responsibility for acts God creates.

Christian theology, particularly following Augustine and Aquinas, developed positions that attempt to preserve both divine omniscience and genuine human free will. God's knowledge is eternal and comprehensive, but this does not negate human freedom. God acts in ways that respect and work through human agency rather than bypassing it.

4. The Status of Jesus: Christianity affirms that Jesus is the Son of God, the second person of the Trinity, truly divine and truly human, the one through whom God was revealed and redemption accomplished. Islam respects Jesus ('Isa) as one of God's greatest prophets and messengers, born of a virgin, performing miracles, and a sign of God's power. However, Islam firmly denies that Jesus is God's son or divine. For Muslims, calling Jesus the "son of God" literalizes what should be understood metaphorically or as a title of honor, contrary to God's absolute transcendence.

5. Salvation and Redemption: Christianity understands salvation as primarily reconciliation with God through Christ's death and resurrection, requiring personal faith and transformation. Islamic theology understands salvation as submission (islam) to God's will and obedience to His commandments. While Islam acknowledges that God forgives sinners who repent, it does not require belief in Christ's redemptive work.

6. Divine Immutability and Temporal Relationship: Both traditions affirm God's immutability (unchangeability), but Christian theology more explicitly grapples with how an unchanging God relates to creation in time. Islamic theology similarly emphasizes divine transcendence but often presents this differently, sometimes suggesting God's will is directly expressed in temporal events.

7. The Problem of God's Transcendence and Immanence: Both traditions must balance God's absolute transcendence with God's active involvement in creation. Islamic theology tends to emphasize transcendence more strongly, maintaining careful distinctions between Creator and creation. Christian theology, particularly through the doctrine of the Incarnation, emphasizes that God enters into history and human experience while remaining transcendent. Muslims might argue this risks compromising God's transcendence; Christians might argue this better expresses God's love.

These distinctions are not matters of mere academic theology but touch on fundamental questions of who God is and how God relates to the world and to humanity. Each tradition believes its understanding best preserves both the truth of God's perfections and coherence with its revealed sources.

sections.title: Internal Diversity Within Each Tradition
sections.body:
Both Islam and Christianity contain significant internal theological diversity on questions concerning God's nature. It is important not to present either tradition as monolithic.

Within Islamic theology, several schools of thought have historically developed different approaches to understanding God's attributes and their relationship to God's essence. The Mu'tazilite school, prominent in early Islamic history, emphasized God's justice and human reason's ability to discern moral truths. They held that God's attributes (justice, knowledge, power) are identical with God's essence to preserve God's absolute unity and avoid suggesting God has multiple components. The Ash'arite school, developed in reaction to Mu'tazilite rationalism, sought to preserve both God's absolute transcendence and the reality of God's attributes as distinct from God's essence, though not as separate beings. Maturidite theology, another major school, offers positions between these approaches.

On questions like divine omniscience and human free will, Islamic theology has proposed different solutions. Some theologians emphasized divine omnipotence so strongly that human choices are seen as ultimately determined by God, though humans acquire moral responsibility through their intentions. Others developed more nuanced positions attempting to preserve genuine human agency. Debates on these questions continue in contemporary Islamic scholarship.

Within Christian theology, equally substantial diversity exists. The Eastern Orthodox tradition emphasizes the apophatic dimension of theology—what cannot be said about God due to God's transcendence—more strongly than Western theology. Orthodox theology distinguishes between God's essence (which is beyond all human knowing) and God's energies (God's activities through which God can be known). This approach attempts to preserve both God's transcendence and the possibility of genuine knowledge of God.

Western Christian theology divides into Catholic and Protestant traditions, each with significant internal diversity. Catholic theology, developed through medieval scholasticism and refined through subsequent centuries, emphasizes natural theology (the ability of human reason to know God through creation and reason) alongside revealed theology. The theological method of Thomas Aquinas remains influential but has been developed and debated by subsequent generations.

Protestant theology, beginning with the Reformation, emphasizes the centrality of scripture and Christ, though Protestant thinkers have approached questions about God's nature through different philosophical frameworks. Reformed theology (following Calvin) has historically emphasized divine sovereignty and predestination more strongly, while Arminian theology attempts to preserve both divine omniscience and human free will more explicitly.

Contemporary Christian theology includes liberation theology, which emphasizes God's justice and solidarity with the oppressed; feminist theology, which questions traditional masculine language for God; process theology, which questions classical attributes like omnipotence and immutability; and various other approaches.

On the question of Christ's divine nature, while Trinitarian Christology is mainstream in Catholic, Orthodox, and most Protestant churches, historical Christian movements like Arianism, Nestorianism, and Monophysitism offered different understandings of how Christ's human and divine natures relate. Modern Christian denominations vary in how literally they affirm Trinitarian formulations.

These internal diversities demonstrate that both traditions are living, developing conversations about God's nature rather than static systems. Both contain resources for different approaches while maintaining core commitments to God's fundamental perfections.

sections.title: Contemporary Scholarship and Dialogue
sections.body:
Modern Islamic and Christian scholarship has engaged increasingly in comparative study of God's nature, seeking both to understand each other's positions more deeply and to address shared philosophical questions.

Contemporary Islamic scholars have brought new philosophical sophistication to classical theological questions. Thinkers like Ali Shariati have explored how Islamic theology speaks to modern concerns about justice and human dignity. Scholars like Abdulaziz Sachedina have developed Islamic approaches to human rights grounded in God's justice and mercy. Other contemporary Muslim philosophers have engaged with process theology, existentialism, and feminist philosophy while remaining grounded in Islamic sources.

Christian scholars have similarly brought contemporary philosophical resources to classical doctrines. Process theology, developed by thinkers like Charles Hartshorne and influenced by process philosophy, has questioned classical notions of God's omnipotence and immutability, suggesting instead a God who is genuinely affected by creation and shares in creation's becoming. While not mainstream, process theology has influenced significant Christian thought.

Feminist theology in Christianity, developed by scholars like Rosemary Radford Ruether and Elisabeth Schüssler Fiorenza, has examined how traditional theological language (particularly masculine imagery for God) has shaped Christian communities and questioned whether classical attributes like omnipotence reflect God's true character or patriarchal projections. Similar questions have been raised in Islamic feminist theology by scholars like Fatima Mernissi and Amina Wadud.

Interfaith scholarship has produced significant works examining divine nature comparatively. Scholars like John Hick have argued that different religious traditions experience and articulate the same ultimate transcendent reality through different cultural and linguistic frameworks. Others like S. Mark Heim have argued that religious traditions have genuinely different conceptions of ultimacy and that dialogue should respect these differences rather than assuming convergence.

Contemporary dialogue between Islamic and Christian thinkers has produced several important points of understanding:

1. Both traditions ground their understanding of God in concrete revelation texts (Qur'an and Bible) rather than abstract philosophy alone, even when employing philosophical frameworks.

2. Both traditions have grappled with the problem of understanding God's transcendence and relationship to creation, using different solutions that reflect different theological priorities.

3. Both traditions emphasize that God's nature exceeds human comprehension, even though both believe genuine knowledge of God is possible and essential.

4. Disagreements on specific doctrines (Trinity, Christology, incarnation) reflect genuine theological differences, not misunderstandings or errors in one party's grasp of the other's position.

5. Common ethical concerns (justice, mercy, compassion, stewardship) flow from shared understanding of divine attributes, even when articulated through different theological frameworks.

Critical scholarship has also noted that some comparative theology projects risk oversimplifying traditions or seeking false convergence. The most rigorous contemporary scholarship acknowledges both real similarities and real differences, allowing each tradition to be heard in its own terms while enabling genuine comparative analysis.

sections.title: Implications for Understanding and Dialogue
sections.body:
The comparative study of God's nature in Islam and Christianity has several important implications.

First, understanding each tradition's internal logic helps prevent caricature. Islam is not merely a simplified monotheism, nor is the Trinity an illogical addition to monotheism. Both reflect coherent theological systems that have developed over centuries, grappling seriously with philosophical challenges and scriptural interpretation.

Second, recognizing both shared convictions and genuine differences enables more productive dialogue. Common ground in affirming God's omniscience, omnipotence, justice, and mercy provides a foundation for conversation. However, pretending that differences do not exist or are merely terminological obscures the real theological claims at stake.

Third, the sophisticated theological resources within each tradition offer value to those seeking to understand God. Islamic philosophy's emphasis on divine simplicity and transcendence, Christian theology's grappling with divine self-giving love and Trinitarian relationality—these represent serious intellectual attempts to preserve both God's perfection and God's relationship to creation.

Fourth, contemporary challenges (from atheism, religious pluralism, scientific worldview, feminist consciousness) are shared across traditions and may benefit from comparative theological resources. Islamic theology's articulation of divine justice alongside mercy, Christian theology's wrestling with suffering and divine involvement in history, Eastern Orthodox theology's apophatic caution—all offer valuable perspectives on perennial theological questions.

Finally, respect for each tradition requires recognizing that disagreements on God's nature are not peripheral but central to each faith's identity and practice. Muslims do not regard the Trinity as a minor refinement but as incompatible with authentic tawhid. Christians do not regard Christology as optional but as central to their understanding of God's redemptive action. Genuine dialogue acknowledges this significance rather than minimizing it. At the same time, both traditions share commitment to God's moral perfection and the possibility of human relationship with God, even as they understand these differently.

quranVerses.arabic: هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ عَٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَٰدَةِ ۖ هُوَ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ
quranVerses.translation: He is Allah, other than whom there is no deity, Knower of the unseen and the witnessed. He is the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَٰمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ ٱلْمُهَيْمِنُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْجَبَّارُ ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرُ ۚ سُبْحَٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ
quranVerses.translation: He is Allah, other than whom there is no deity, the Sovereign, the Pure, the Perfection, the Bestower of Faith, the Overseer, the Exalted in Might, the Compeller, the Superior. Exalted is Allah above whatever they associate with Him.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَٰلِقُ ٱلْبَارِئُ ٱلْمُصَوِّرُ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ
quranVerses.translation: He is Allah, the Creator, the Inventor, the Fashioner; to Him belong the best names. Whatever is in the heavens and earth is exalting Him. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ
quranVerses.translation: Allah - there is no deity except Him. To Him belong the best names.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ ۚ لَا تَأْخُذُهُۥ سِنَةٌۭ وَلَا نَوْمٌۭ ۚ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ مَن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يَشْفَعُ عِندَهُۥٓ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۚ يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَىْءٍۢ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّا بِمَا شَآءَ ۚ وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ ۖ وَلَا يَـُٔودُهُۥ حِفْظُهُمَا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْعَظِيمُ
quranVerses.translation: Allah - there is no deity except Him, the Ever-Living, the Sustainer of [all] existence. Neither drowsiness overtakes Him nor sleep. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission? He knows what is [presently] before them and what will be after them, and they encompass not a thing of His knowledge except for what He wills. His Kursi extends over the heavens and the earth, and their preservation tires Him not. And He is the Most High, the Most Great.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: He who doesn’t love doesn’t know God, for God is love.
Now to the King eternal, immortal, invisible, to God who alone is wise, be honor and glory forever and ever. Amen.

In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. The same was in the beginning with God. All things were made through him. Without him was not anything made that has been made.
Therefore you shall be perfect, just as your Father in heaven is perfect.

faq.question: Do Islam and Christianity worship the same God?
faq.answer: Both Islam and Christianity are monotheistic religions affirming one God. Muslims and Christians share belief in God's fundamental attributes: omniscience, omnipotence, justice, mercy, and eternity. However, they differ significantly on God's nature and self-revelation. Islam emphasizes God's absolute oneness (tawhid) and rejects the Christian doctrine of the Trinity and the belief that God became incarnate in Jesus. Christianity understands God as Trinitarian (Father, Son, Holy Spirit) and affirms Jesus as God's Word made flesh. These are not minor disagreements about terminology but genuine theological differences about who God is and how God relates to creation. Some Christian and Muslim theologians argue this represents different understanding of the same God; others emphasize that the differences are substantial enough to constitute different conceptions of God's very nature.

faq.question: What are the 99 Names of Allah and what is their significance?
faq.answer: The 99 Names of Allah (Al-Asma al-Husna) are divine names and attributes revealed in the Qur'an that describe different dimensions of God's nature and relationship to creation. These include names describing God's power (Al-Qawi—The Strong), knowledge (Al-Alim—The All-Knowing), mercy (Ar-Rahman—The Merciful), justice (Al-Adl—The Just), and transcendence (Al-Aliyy—The Most High). Islamic theology emphasizes that while these names describe different aspects of God, they all express a single, unified divine reality. Knowing and reflecting on God's names is considered central to Islamic spiritual practice. Muslims often recite and meditate on the names, and Islamic theology teaches that understanding these names helps believers develop a deeper relationship with God and align their own character with divine attributes.

faq.question: How do Islam and Christianity understand God's transcendence differently?
faq.answer: Both traditions affirm God's transcendence—God is beyond creation, not limited by time, space, or physical laws, and fundamentally different from created beings. However, Islamic theology tends to emphasize transcendence more strongly, maintaining careful distinctions between Creator and creation. Islamic philosophy developed sophisticated arguments for God's absolute uniqueness (tawhid) and God's radical difference from all that is created. Christian theology equally affirms God's transcendence but balances it differently through the doctrine of the Incarnation, which affirms that God became human in Jesus Christ. Islamic theology regards this as a threat to God's transcendence, arguing that God cannot become bound by human limitations without losing God's essential nature. Christian theology argues that God's self-giving love and entry into human history represents the fullest expression of God's transcendence—God is free enough to enter creation and involved enough to suffer with it.

faq.question: What is the Trinity, and why do Muslims reject it?
faq.answer: The Trinity is the Christian doctrine that the one God exists eternally as three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Christians maintain that this is strict monotheism—there is only one God—but that this one God's nature is internally relational and Trinitarian. Muslims reject the Trinity as incompatible with authentic monotheism (tawhid). From the Islamic perspective, saying God is three (or that God is one and three simultaneously) introduces multiplication into God's nature, which violates the principle of divine oneness and uniqueness. The Qur'an explicitly rejects trinitarian conceptions and warns against associating anything with God (shirk). Christian theology responds that the Trinity is not tritheism but a single, undivided divine essence subsisting eternally as three persons—Father, Son, and Holy Spirit are not three separate beings or three gods, and orthodox Trinitarian doctrine (as formulated at Nicaea and Constantinople) explicitly anathematizes any reading that would multiply the divine essence; on this view, the distinction of persons concerns relations within the one God rather than a division or addition to God's substance. Muslim theology maintains that this distinction, however carefully stated, does not resolve the underlying theological problem: God should not be described as three in any sense, as this compromises God's absolute uniqueness and unity.

faq.question: How do both traditions understand God's justice and mercy?
faq.answer: Both Islam and Christianity affirm that God is both perfectly just and perfectly merciful, though they articulate the relationship between these attributes somewhat differently. Islamic theology emphasizes that God is Al-Adl (The Just) and simultaneously Ar-Rahman and Ar-Rahim (The Merciful, The Compassionate). Islamic philosophy addresses potential tensions by affirming that God's justice and mercy are unified in God's higher wisdom that humans cannot fully comprehend. Christian theology similarly holds that God is just and merciful, particularly emphasizing that divine mercy and justice are expressed through Christ's redemptive work. In Christian understanding, Christ's death and resurrection represent God's justice (requiring atonement for sin) and mercy (providing forgiveness freely through grace) working together. While the traditions differ in how they understand salvation and atonement, both maintain that God's justice and compassion are complementary expressions of divine perfection rather than contradictory attributes.

faq.question: Do Islam and Christianity have different understandings of God's attributes?
faq.answer: Both traditions affirm core divine attributes: omniscience, omnipotence, justice, mercy, eternity, and transcendence. However, there are differences in emphasis, philosophical articulation, and theological implications. Islamic theology developed sophisticated philosophical positions on the relationship between God's essence and attributes, with major schools (Mu'tazilite, Ash'arite, Maturidite) offering different solutions. Christian theology, particularly in Western scholasticism, also engaged deeply with questions about God's attributes and their relationship to God's essence. A key difference concerns God's relationship to creation and time: Islamic theology often emphasizes God's absolute sovereignty and the determination of all events, while Christian theology attempts to balance divine omniscience with genuine human free will. Additionally, Christian theology uniquely must explain how God's attributes relate to the Trinity and the Incarnation—how the attributes of the Father also belong to the Son and Holy Spirit, and how God became human in Christ while remaining divine. Both traditions maintain that God's attributes are ultimately unified in God's perfect nature, even though human understanding struggles to comprehend this unity.

furtherReading.title: Al Tawhid: Its Implications for Thought and Life
furtherReading.author: Ismail Raji al-Faruqi
furtherReading.note: A widely cited philosophical and theological exploration of tawhid as the organizing principle of Islamic thought, examining its implications for metaphysics, ethics, and society.

furtherReading.title: Summa Theologiae, Prima Pars, Questions 3-14
furtherReading.author: Thomas Aquinas
furtherReading.note: Classical Christian theological treatment of divine attributes, omniscience, omnipotence, and simplicity from medieval Catholic theology.

furtherReading.title: Knowing the Unknowable God: Ibn-Sina, Maimonides, Aquinas
furtherReading.author: David Burrell
furtherReading.note: Comparative analysis of how Islamic and Christian theology understand God's attributes and the limits of human knowledge of the divine.

furtherReading.title: God, Evil and the Limits of Theology
furtherReading.author: Karen Kilby
furtherReading.note: Contemporary Christian theological work examining divine nature, theodicy, and the doctrine of the Trinity.
